pages.raw_text:
The Press today took up with
Mayor Moore and E. J. Cannon,
counsel for the Northern Pacific,
the matter of securing a guarantee
in the ordinance permitting ele-
waited tracks through this city, that
white labor will be employed and
Spkane residents favored whenever
possible.


Mayor Moore favored the plan
and said he would use his influence
to have such a condition incorpo-
rated in the ordinance.


The Press cannot speak for the
councilmen individually, but has no
doubt but that the value of winning
the 100000 and more to be ex-
pended on the work for local labor
will instantly appeal to them.
Mr.. Cannon said that this phase
of the matter had never before been
mentioned to him and he could give
no official assurance on the sub
ject. The work will undoubtedly
be let to contract in the company's
usual manner. On construction be-
tween this city and Portland white
labor is generally employed by the
contractors.


From the company's viewpoint it
is now too late to take up this pro-
vision. 1t is not too late, however,
for the city to take 1t up and insist
upon its incorporation in the ordi-
nance. Such provision appeals d.
rectly to business men as well as
laborers. 1t means a great element
of local prosperity and 1t is not be-
yond what the city has the right to
ask in return for concessions grant
ed.


While the strict legality of such
clause might be subject to attack,
it's moral weight is one that cannot
be overlooked. In the event of its
adoption both city and company are
certain to get value received.
If Aslatic labor is employed it
means a heavy loss in currency cir
culation and the invasion of UNI
desirable foreigners to be pastured
on the city until the work is COM
pleted.


One of the strong points made by
G.. b. Dennis last nigh against
Judge Turner's argument hat the


beauty of the city would be married
| by elevated tracks was that Spo-
kane must depend for supremacy


and progress on commercial de-


velopment. and commercial develop
ment is impossible without rail-
